persona.name: Priya Iyer
persona.education: B.Tech. Computer Science, IIT Madras. Twelve years building backend systems for B2B SaaS companies.
long_term_goals: Run her startup's production workload across multiple machines without ever standing up a Kubernetes cluster
Build a backend stack that one engineer can fully understand and operate on call
Achieve regional redundancy without paying for a multi-region managed platform

occupation.title: Founding Backend Engineer
occupation.description: First and currently only backend engineer at a seed-stage startup building a workflow automation product. Owns the backend, infrastructure, and on-call rotation for production. Reports to the technical co-founder.

persona.communication_style: Calm, careful, slightly understated. Defaults to whiteboard sketches and protocol diagrams when explaining architecture. Allergic to hype.

traits: Believes most production outages come from accidentally complex infrastructure
Will read a wire protocol spec end to end before adopting a runtime
Optimizes for the smallest set of moving parts she can defend on call

big_five_traits.openness: High — actively seeks out unusual but well-engineered runtimes
big_five_traits.conscientiousness: High — runs personal post-mortems after every production incident
big_five_traits.extraversion: Low-moderate — prefers async writing over meetings
big_five_traits.agreeableness: Moderate — collegial but unwilling to inherit infrastructure she does not understand
big_five_traits.neuroticism: Low — long on-call experience has made her steady

likes: Encrypted mesh networking between her own machines
Runtimes with a documented binary wire protocol
Declarative placement constraints she can read at a glance
dislikes: Kubernetes for workloads that do not need it
Hidden control planes she cannot inspect
Hand-rolled TLS termination across multiple boxes
Operational complexity she cannot explain to her co-founder

skills: Linux systems administration on bare metal and small VMs
beliefs: A small startup can run a multi-machine production cluster without Kubernetes if the runtime is designed for it
Mesh-style peer-to-peer architectures beat hub-and-spoke for small clusters where every node matters
Encryption between her own machines is non-negotiable, even on a private network

projects.title: Tessellate Production Backend
projects.description: The production backend for Tessellate's workflow automation product. Currently runs across three small VMs in two regions, hand-glued together with WireGuard, systemd, Postgres replication, and a homemade deploy script. Pain points: every component is a different operational story, encryption between machines is her own configuration, deploys are not atomic.
key_contributions: Designed the WireGuard mesh that links the three production VMs
Wrote the deployment script and incident runbooks the rest of the team relies on

general: Reads distributed systems papers and runtime postmortems for fun
Maintains a personal notebook of every production incident she has ever run
Refuses to adopt a runtime whose failure modes she cannot enumerate

morning: Reviews overnight alerts and the deploy log
Checks her three production nodes' status by hand before standup
workday: Codes backend features in two-hour blocks
Pairs with the founding frontend engineer on cross-cutting designs
evening: Reads infrastructure blogs and protocol specifications
Tinkers with her home lab of small ARM boxes
weekend: Runs experiments with new runtimes on her home lab before bringing anything near production

relationships.name: Rohan Mehta
relationships.description: Technical co-founder; trusts Priya entirely with backend and infrastructure decisions and asks her to keep the on-call story sustainable.

relationships.name: Founding team (8 people)
relationships.description: Small founding team; everyone takes some on-call shifts but Priya owns the backend tier.

other_facts: current_stack: Three small VMs across two regions, glued together with WireGuard, Postgres streaming replication, systemd-managed Node.js services, and a homemade bash deploy script.
current_stack: Operates the cluster entirely by hand without Kubernetes, Nomad, or any orchestrator.
jobs_to_be_done: Run the backend across a small number of coordinated machines with encrypted communication between them.
jobs_to_be_done: Express placement constraints (zones, replicas, anti-affinity) declaratively without writing an operator.
jobs_to_be_done: Survive single-machine failure without manual intervention.
pains: Every distributed-systems primitive she relies on is a different tool with its own configuration and failure modes.
pains: Encryption between her own machines is her own responsibility and her own bug surface.
pains: Adding a fourth machine to her cluster today would require rewriting most of her tooling.
adoption_yes_if: freq-cloud's encrypted UDP mesh handles inter-node communication for her without WireGuard.
adoption_yes_if: She can declare placement (zones, replicas, anti-affinity) in a manifest the runtime actually enforces.
adoption_yes_if: The runtime idles small enough to run on her existing VM sizes without resizing.
adoption_yes_if: There is a documented wire protocol she can read end to end before betting production on it.
rejection_no_if: The runtime requires a separate orchestrator or control plane she cannot operate herself.
rejection_no_if: Cross-node behavior is undocumented or only works on a clean cluster.
rejection_no_if: She cannot enumerate the runtime's failure modes from its source.
anti_goals: She does not want to scale beyond a handful of machines.
anti_goals: She does not need a managed service or a vendor relationship.
anti_goals: She is uninterested in fancy autoscaling or massive multi-tenant features.
recognition_cues: Phrases like 'three VMs in two regions', 'small HA cluster', 'no Kubernetes', 'mesh between my own machines', 'I want to read the wire protocol'.
recognition_cues: Asks about exact failover behavior, leader election timing, and reconciliation intervals.
recognition_cues: Treats encryption between her own nodes as a baseline expectation, not a feature.
